title: Topic 197: The Psychology of Gaming Podcasts: Audio Content for Gamers
content:
# Topic 197: The Psychology of Gaming Podcasts: Audio Content for Gamers

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the peculiar, and frankly, quite nerdy world of gaming podcasts, a digital phenomenon where people willingly spend hours listening to other people talk about playing video games. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this curious pastime, from the formation of one-sided “friendships” with hosts who don’t know you exist (a phenomenon politely termed “parasocial relationships”), to the insatiable human need for information, even if that information is about the optimal DPS build for a fictional elf. Through a rigorous, albeit occasionally satirical, analysis of existing research and real-world examples, we argue that gaming podcasts are more than just background noise for doing the dishes. They are a fascinating case study in community formation, consumer behavior, and the ever-blurring lines between entertainment, social connection, and having a socially acceptable excuse to think about dragons all day.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand, cacophonous bazaar of the internet, where cat videos reign supreme and attention spans are measured in nanoseconds, a curious creature has emerged and thrived: the gaming podcast. This is a medium where grown adults, with mortgages and responsibilities, dedicate significant portions of their week to listening to other adults discuss, dissect, and occasionally despair over the digital worlds of video games. To the uninitiated, this might seem as baffling as trying to explain the offside rule to a goldfish. Why would anyone spend their precious free time listening to a conversation they can’t even participate in, about a hobby that is, byits very nature, interactive and visual? Is it a sign of a burgeoning new form of entertainment, a desperate cry for connection in an increasingly isolated world, or simply what happens when you run out of shows to binge on Netflix?

This paper posits that the answer is, of course, a little bit of all of the above, with a healthy dose of psychological shenanigans thrown in for good measure. We will embark on a thrilling expedition into the minds of the gaming podcast listener, a demographic that, as we will see, is driven by a complex cocktail of informational needs, social desires, and the comforting illusion of friendship with a disembodied voice. We will explore how these podcasts, in their own quirky way, are reshaping our understanding of media consumption, community building, and the very nature of entertainment itself. From the bizarrely intimate bond listeners form with their favorite hosts, to the way these audio-only experiences can enhance a visual medium, we will uncover the surprisingly profound reasons why millions of people are plugging in and tuning out the real world, in favor of a conversation about a virtual one. So, grab your headphones, suspend your disbelief, and prepare to question everything you thought you knew about the simple act of listening.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

To suggest that the academic world has been slow on the uptake when it comes to the scholarly study of gaming podcasts would be an understatement of colossal proportions. While researchers have been diligently dissecting every other conceivable form of media, from the semiotics of silent films to the societal impact of TikTok dances, the humble gaming podcast has largely been left to languish in the unexamined corners of the internet. However, what little research does exist provides a fascinating, if not slightly comical, glimpse into the psyche of the modern gamer. The prevailing theoretical lens through which scholars have attempted to understand this phenomenon is the “uses and gratifications” theory, a rather grandiloquent term for what essentially boils down to “what do people get out of this stuff?” [1] [2].

The pioneering work of Tobin and Guadagno (2022) in their whimsically titled paper, “Why people listen,” provides a foundational understanding of podcast consumption in general. Their findings suggest that, contrary to what one might assume, the primary driver for tuning into podcasts is not a desperate need for social connection, but rather a voracious appetite for information [1]. It seems that the modern human, when faced with the mundane reality of their daily commute, would rather learn about the intricacies of 18th-century naval warfare or, in our case, the optimal loot-farming route in *Diablo IV*, than listen to the same four songs on the radio for the thousandth time. This thirst for knowledge, this “need for cognition,” as the academics so charmingly put it, is a key predictor of who is most likely to become a podcast aficionado [1].

However, the informational aspect is only half of the story. The true psychological intrigue lies in the bizarrely one-sided relationships that listeners form with podcast hosts, a phenomenon known as “parasocial relationships.” This is where a listener, after hours of exposure to a host’s voice, begins to feel as though they know them personally, despite the fact that the host is blissfully unaware of their existence. It’s like having a best friend who never asks you to help them move. The research of Stanton (2025) delves into this very specific corner of the gaming world, proposing the concept of a “one-and-a-quarter way relationship” [2]. This is a subtle but crucial distinction from the more interactive “one-and-a-half way relationship” seen on platforms like Twitch, where viewers can directly interact with streamers in real-time. In the world of gaming podcasts, the lack of immediate, constant interaction makes the moments of connection—a host reading a listener’s email, for example—feel more deliberate, more earned, and therefore, more “authentic” [2]. This perceived authenticity, this feeling that you’re listening to a genuine conversation between friends, is the secret sauce that keeps listeners coming back for more, even when they know, on a rational level, that they are essentially eavesdropping on a conversation they’ll never be a part of.

## ANALYSIS

Having waded through the surprisingly shallow pool of existing literature, we now arrive at the analytical core of our investigation. Here, we shall attempt to dissect the gaming podcast listener with the precision of a surgeon and the gentle, mocking tone of a particularly witty talk show host. What, precisely, is happening in the brain of someone who willingly subjects themselves to hundreds of hours of discussion about virtual worlds? The answer, we propose, lies in a holy trinity of psychological phenomena: **The Benevolent Overlord Host, The Cult of Authenticity, and The Imaginary Water Cooler.**

First, let us consider **The Benevolent Overlord Host**. This is the podcast host who, through the magical intimacy of the audio medium, transcends their role as a mere content creator and ascends to the status of a trusted friend, an older sibling, or perhaps even a wise, albeit slightly goofy, mentor. This is the parasocial relationship in its most potent form. The listener, often engaged in the soul-crushingly mundane tasks of daily life – commuting, cleaning, pretending to work – invites the host’s voice directly into their prefrontal cortex. Over time, this consistent, one-way intimacy creates a powerful bond. The host’s running gags become the listener’s in-jokes. Their opinions on the latest game release become the listener’s own, to be regurgitated at the next available social gathering. This is not merely about information; it is about companionship. The host provides a sense of social connection without the messy, unpredictable, and frankly, exhausting, demands of a real-life friendship. It is the social equivalent of a Tamagotchi: all the warm, fuzzy feelings of nurturing a relationship, with none of the actual responsibility.

This leads us directly to our second pillar: **The Cult of Authenticity**. In a world saturated with slick, corporate-sponsored content, the gaming podcast often presents itself as a bastion of genuine, unscripted conversation. The occasional verbal stumble, the off-topic tangent about a particularly delicious sandwich, the audible clinking of a coffee mug – these are not imperfections; they are features. They are the hallmarks of authenticity, the little breadcrumbs of reality that convince the listener they are privy to a genuine conversation between friends. As Stanton’s (2025) work suggests, the very lack of direct, constant interaction in the podcasting medium enhances this perception of authenticity [2]. A host who takes the time to read a listener’s email is not just filling airtime; they are bestowing a moment of genuine, focused attention, a rare and precious commodity in the digital age. This curated authenticity is a powerful driver of consumer behavior. Listeners are not just consuming content; they are buying into a relationship, a brand, a personality. And when that personality recommends a new game, a piece of hardware, or a particularly comfortable gaming chair, the listener is far more likely to open their wallet than they would be for a traditional advertisement. It’s not a sales pitch; it’s a recommendation from a “friend.”

Finally, we have **The Imaginary Water Cooler**. Humans are, by nature, social creatures. We have a deep-seated need to share our experiences, to validate our opinions, and to feel like we are part of a tribe. Gaming, while often a solitary activity, is no exception. The gaming podcast provides a virtual water cooler around which listeners can gather. It gives them a shared language, a common set of references, and a sense of belonging to a community of like-minded individuals. When a host spends an hour dissecting the lore of *Elden Ring*, they are not just talking about a video game; they are providing their listeners with the social currency they need to participate in the broader gaming conversation. The listener who can confidently quote a podcast’s take on a particular game is not just showing off their knowledge; they are signaling their membership in the tribe. This sense of community is a powerful motivator, and it is a key reason why listeners will not only tolerate, but actively seek out, hours upon hours of what is, in essence, a conversation they are not a part of. They are not just listening; they are participating, vicariously, in a community that understands their passion, validates their hobby, and, most importantly, gets the joke.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

To prevent this paper from ascending entirely into the ethereal realm of abstract theory, let us ground our analysis in the fertile soil of real-world examples. These are the stories, the legends, the cautionary tales from the front lines of gaming podcast fandom.

Consider, for instance, the case of the “Giant Bombcast,” a long-running and much-beloved institution in the gaming podcast landscape. The hosts of the Giant Bombcast are not merely journalists; they are, in the minds of their listeners, a surrogate group of friends. The podcast has been running for so long that many listeners have, in a very real sense, grown up with the hosts. They have been there for marriages, for births, for career changes, for the slow, inexorable march of time and the ever-increasing encroachment of grey hairs. When a long-time host, Brad Shoemaker, announced his departure from the site, the outpouring of grief from the community was palpable. Grown men, with beards and 401ks, took to the internet to express a sense of loss that was, by any rational measure, completely disproportionate to the situation. One listener, in a now-infamous Reddit post, confessed to his wife that he was “sad because his friend was moving away,” only to have to awkwardly explain that this “friend” was a man he had never met, who lived on the other side of the country, and whose primary contribution to his life was a weekly discussion about the relative merits of different video game consoles. This, in a nutshell, is the power of the Benevolent Overlord Host.

Then there is the curious case of “The Adventure Zone,” an actual-play Dungeons & Dragons podcast hosted by the McElroy brothers and their father. While not a traditional gaming podcast, it is a masterclass in the Cult of Authenticity. The McElroys have built an empire on a foundation of self-deprecating humor, a willingness to be vulnerable, and a complete and utter lack of pretense. They are not voice actors; they are three brothers and their dad, fumbling their way through a fantasy world, and the result is utterly charming. The authenticity of their familial bond is the secret ingredient that has cultivated one of the most passionate and dedicated fanbases in all of podcasting. The internet is littered with stories of fans who have been profoundly impacted by the show, from getting tattoos of their favorite characters, to naming their pets after the show’s bumbling heroes. There is a particularly heartwarming, and frankly, quite bizarre, story of a young woman who, when asked in a job interview to name a person she admired, unironically and at great length, described the character of “Taako,” a flamboyant, taco-loving elf wizard from the podcast. She did not get the job, but her unwavering commitment to her fictional hero is a testament to the power of a well-crafted, authentic narrative.

Finally, let us examine the “Kinda Funny Gamescast” as a prime example of the Imaginary Water Cooler. The hosts of Kinda Funny have built their entire brand around the concept of being your “best friends” in the gaming industry. Their shows are heavily reliant on audience participation, with listener-submitted questions and topics driving much of the conversation. This creates a powerful sense of community, a feeling that you are not just a passive listener, but an active participant in the conversation. The Kinda Funny community has its own language, its own inside jokes, its own shared history. To be a fan of Kinda Funny is to be a part of a club, and the podcast is the weekly clubhouse meeting. There is a (possibly apocryphal) story of a young man who, during a particularly stressful job interview, was asked to describe a time he had to work as part of a team. He launched into a detailed explanation of how he and his fellow “best friends” in the Kinda Funny community had once successfully predicted the entire lineup of a major video game conference, a feat of collaborative deduction that, in his mind, was a shining example of teamwork and strategic thinking. He, too, did not get the job, but his story serves as a perfect illustration of how a gaming podcast can provide a sense of community and shared purpose that is, for many, just as real and just as meaningful as any real-world interaction.

## DISCUSSION

What, then, are we to make of this strange and wonderful world of gaming podcasts? What are the broader implications of this phenomenon for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits? The most immediate, and perhaps most unsettling, conclusion is that the line between entertainment and social connection has become so hopelessly blurred as to be almost meaningless. The gaming podcast is not just a new form of media; it is a new form of social interaction, a low-stakes, low-effort alternative to the messy, unpredictable, and often disappointing world of real-life relationships. This has profound implications for how we think about community, friendship, and the very nature of human connection in the digital age.

From a consumer behavior perspective, the gaming podcast represents a paradigm shift in how brands and advertisers can reach their target audience. The traditional, interruptive model of advertising is becoming increasingly ineffective in a world where consumers have more control over their media consumption than ever before. The gaming podcast, with its emphasis on authenticity and its cultivation of parasocial relationships, offers a far more subtle, and far more effective, approach. A recommendation from a trusted podcast host is not an advertisement; it is a piece of friendly advice. A sponsored segment is not a commercial; it is a conversation with a brand that “gets it.” This is the holy grail of marketing: the ability to sell a product without the consumer ever feeling like they are being sold to. As more and more companies begin to understand the power of this new form of “influencer” marketing, we can expect to see a significant shift in how the video game industry, and indeed, many other industries, approach their advertising strategies.

Furthermore, the rise of the gaming podcast challenges our traditional understanding of what it means to be a “fan.” The gaming podcast listener is not a passive consumer of content; they are an active participant in a community, a co-creator of meaning, a keeper of the flame. They are, in a very real sense, a part of the show. This has significant implications for how we think about intellectual property, community management, and the relationship between creators and their audiences. The gaming podcast is not a one-way street; it is a conversation, and the most successful podcasts are the ones that understand and embrace this new, more collaborative, model of media creation.

## CONCLUSION

In the final analysis, the psychology of gaming podcasts is a rich and surprisingly complex field of study. It is a world where the lines between friendship and fandom, between entertainment and social connection, are not just blurred, but gleefully erased. The gaming podcast listener is not just a passive consumer of media; they are an active participant in a new kind of community, a new kind of conversation, a new kind of friendship. They are the pioneers of a new social landscape, one where the most meaningful relationships in your life might just be with a group of people you’ve never met, who are talking about a world that doesn’t even exist. And while the uninitiated may scoff, and the academics may scratch their heads, the gaming podcast listener will just keep on listening, secure in the knowledge that they are part of something special, something unique, something that, in the end, is just a whole lot of fun. And really, what more could you ask for from a hobby?

## REFERENCES

[1] Tobin, S. J., & Guadagno, R. E. (2022). Why people listen: Motivations and outcomes of podcast listening. *PLOS ONE*, *17*(4), e0265806. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0265806

[2] Stanton, R. (2025). “One-and-a-Quarter Way Relationships”: Understanding Authenticity and Asymmetry In Gaming Podcast Audiences. *Proceedings of DiGRA 2025*.
